text: The surgeon carefully mobilizes the bowel, which is adherent to the pelvic sidewall, away from the ovary. The bowel is visible on the right side of the image, while the ovary is on the left. The bowel has been slightly mobilized, but further separation is needed to prevent injury.

The surgeon has mobilized the bowel to reveal a deep pelvic nodule. The nodule is delineated from the pelvic floor muscles visible at the bottom of the image, with the bowel on the right and the nodule at the top. The nodule extends into the vagina, requiring vaginal opening for removal. The dissection shows the ureter and nerves free from the nodule, which has been completely excised.

The surgeon excises a large nodule from the rectum, entering the rectal lumen to ensure complete removal. The excised nodule is then removed and prepared for pathology. The surgical team meticulously closes the defect in the rectum following the excision.
The surgeon performs the second layer of suturing on the bowel, demonstrating a multi-layer closure technique due to the large size of the lesion.

The surgeon ensures the suture line is clean and meticulously completes the bowel anastomosis. An EEA sizer is passed through the bowel to check for leaks, with attention to any air bubbles indicating potential issues.

The surgeon performs an appendectomy on a patient with endometriosis of the appendix. The fat and blood supply to the appendix are separated from its underside up to the base where it attaches to the large bowel. Three endo loops are placed to occlude the appendix proximally, with two loops near the large bowel and one loop on the appendix being removed. The appendix is then separated between these loops.
The surgeon transects the appendix, preparing it for removal. The appendix will be sent to pathology for examination.

The video shows endometriosis affecting the left ureter, with a visible stricture in the middle of the image. The surgeon attempts to pass a stent through the ureter but is unable due to the pronounced stricture, necessitating the excision of the affected area to preserve kidney function.

Urine is visible coming out of the proximal ureter, which is patent just above the stricture. The surgeon uses scissors to avoid thermal damage while spatulating the ureter, enlarging the opening to reduce the risk of stricture during healing.

The surgeon places an absorbable suture to re-approximate the two ends of the ureter, avoiding the use of energy to promote healing. The suture is tied multiple times to secure the approximation. A stent is then inserted through the bladder and passed from the lower edge of the ureter to the upper one, visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon places several absorbable sutures to close the ureter circumferentially, tying them laparoscopically. A stent is visible in place within the ureter, intended to remain for approximately six weeks. After completing the repair, a cystoscopy is performed for a thorough examination of the surgical site.

The video shows endometriosis of the bladder, visible as a purple lesion on cystoscopy, located in front of the uterus. The surgeon separates adhesions and defines a plane between the uterus, cervix, and bladder, mobilizing the bladder away from the uterus and vagina.

The surgeon excises the lesion from the bladder using a harmonic scalpel. The lesion penetrates the entire bladder wall, necessitating entry into the bladder and enlarging the hole to fully remove the lesion. Cystoscopic control is used, with a camera inside the bladder, to ensure complete removal of the lesion.

The Foley catheter is visible inside the bladder following the excision of the lesion. The entrances of the ureters into the bladder are observed on the right and left sides, confirming that the lesion was not located near these sites.

The surgeon completes the second layer of laparoscopic suturing to repair the bladder, using standard absorbable sutures to ensure no leakage. A catheter is placed for seven to ten days to facilitate healing.

The surgeon fills the bladder to check for leakage, performing a cystoscopy to inspect the suture line. An excised endometriosis lesion from the diaphragm is visible, with the lung seen in the background. Sutures are placed to close the area, while thoracic surgeons explore the thoracic cavity concurrently.
